questions.prompt: How does a Docker image differ from a container?

answers.text: Multiple containers can be created from one image.

feedback.legit: Multiple containers can be created from one image. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: Multiple containers can be created from one image. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: An image is an immutable template used to create containers.

feedback.legit: An image is an immutable template used to create containers. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: An image is an immutable template used to create containers. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: A container is a runtime instance created from an image.

feedback.legit: A container is a runtime instance created from an image. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: A container is a runtime instance created from an image. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: To persist data outside a container's writable layer.

feedback.legit: To persist data outside a container's writable layer. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: To persist data outside a container's writable layer. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: To bake source code permanently into an image.

feedback.legit: Image build instructions add files to images; volumes hold runtime data. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: Image build instructions add files to images; volumes hold runtime data. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: A volume can survive deletion of the container that used it.

feedback.legit: A volume can survive deletion of the container that used it. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: A volume can survive deletion of the container that used it. This one was safe to mark Legit.

questions.prompt: Does exposing a port in a Dockerfile publish it to the host?

answers.text: `docker run -p` can map a host port to a container port.

feedback.legit: `docker run -p` can map a host port to a container port. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: `docker run -p` can map a host port to a container port. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: A port must appear in EXPOSE before `docker run -p` can publish it.

feedback.legit: Runtime port publishing can map a container port even when the Dockerfile does not declare EXPOSE. The original reply was the misconception in the thread.
feedback.sus: Runtime port publishing can map a container port even when the Dockerfile does not declare EXPOSE. Your vote matches the correction.

answers.text: Yes, EXPOSE automatically creates a host mapping.

feedback.legit: Publishing requires a runtime port mapping; EXPOSE is metadata/documentation. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: Publishing requires a runtime port mapping; EXPOSE is metadata/documentation. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

questions.prompt: Why copy dependency manifests before application source in many Dockerfiles?

answers.text: A change to an earlier layer invalidates reuse of later layers.

feedback.legit: A change to an earlier layer invalidates reuse of later layers. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: A change to an earlier layer invalidates reuse of later layers. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: The dependency-install layer can remain cached when only source files change.

feedback.legit: The dependency-install layer can remain cached when only source files change. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: The dependency-install layer can remain cached when only source files change. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: The ordering is a cache optimization, not a network rule.

feedback.legit: The ordering is a cache optimization, not a network rule. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: The ordering is a cache optimization, not a network rule. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

questions.prompt: How does a bind mount differ from a named volume?

answers.text: A bind mount maps a specific host path into the container.

feedback.legit: A bind mount maps a specific host path into the container. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: A bind mount maps a specific host path into the container. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: A bind mount is stored only inside the image.

feedback.legit: Bind mounts reference host filesystem paths at runtime. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: Bind mounts reference host filesystem paths at runtime. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: Named volumes are managed through Docker's volume storage.

feedback.legit: Named volumes are managed through Docker's volume storage. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: Named volumes are managed through Docker's volume storage. There was no false claim to flag here.

questions.prompt: Why use a multi-stage Docker build?

answers.text: Every stage runs as a separate production container.

feedback.legit: Stages are build phases; only selected outputs need to reach the final image. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: Stages are build phases; only selected outputs need to reach the final image. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: A multi-stage build automatically updates every base image to its newest version.

feedback.legit: Base-image selection and update policy remain explicit build concerns. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: Base-image selection and update policy remain explicit build concerns. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: Build tools can stay in an earlier stage while only runtime artifacts enter the final image.

feedback.legit: Build tools can stay in an earlier stage while only runtime artifacts enter the final image. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: Build tools can stay in an earlier stage while only runtime artifacts enter the final image. The original wording holds up.

questions.prompt: Why does PID 1 behavior matter inside a container?

answers.text: An init process can help with signal forwarding and zombie reaping.

feedback.legit: An init process can help with signal forwarding and zombie reaping. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: An init process can help with signal forwarding and zombie reaping. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: The main process needs correct signal handling and must reap child processes when it creates them.

feedback.legit: The main process needs correct signal handling and must reap child processes when it creates them. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: The main process needs correct signal handling and must reap child processes when it creates them. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: PID 1 cannot receive termination signals.

feedback.legit: PID 1 can receive signals, but the process and entrypoint must forward or handle them correctly. The original reply was the misconception in the thread.
feedback.sus: PID 1 can receive signals, but the process and entrypoint must forward or handle them correctly. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

questions.prompt: Is an image environment variable a safe place for a permanent secret?

answers.text: Yes, image configuration is invisible to anyone who pulls it.

feedback.legit: Image metadata and layers can expose embedded values. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: Image metadata and layers can expose embedded values. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

answers.text: Secrets should be injected through an appropriate runtime secret mechanism.

feedback.legit: Secrets should be injected through an appropriate runtime secret mechanism. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: Secrets should be injected through an appropriate runtime secret mechanism. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: Removing a secret in a later layer may not remove it from earlier image history.

feedback.legit: Removing a secret in a later layer may not remove it from earlier image history. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: Removing a secret in a later layer may not remove it from earlier image history. This was accurate as written.

questions.prompt: What does a Docker health check indicate?

answers.text: That every downstream dependency is permanently available.

feedback.legit: A health command only tests what it was designed to observe. The original reply was the misconception in the thread.
feedback.sus: A health command only tests what it was designed to observe. Sus was the correct call here.

answers.text: A Docker health check by itself always restarts an unhealthy container.

feedback.legit: A health check reports status; restart behavior requires an orchestrator or other policy. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: A health check reports status; restart behavior requires an orchestrator or other policy. The Sus label fits the misconception.

answers.text: Whether a configured command considers the container healthy.

feedback.legit: Whether a configured command considers the container healthy. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Whether a configured command considers the container healthy. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

questions.prompt: Does basic Compose `depends_on` prove a dependency is ready to serve requests?

answers.text: Health-based conditions can improve orchestration when supported and configured.

feedback.legit: Health-based conditions can improve orchestration when supported and configured. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: Health-based conditions can improve orchestration when supported and configured. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: Yes, process start order guarantees application readiness.

feedback.legit: A started container may still be initializing; readiness needs a health condition or retry logic. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: A started container may still be initializing; readiness needs a health condition or retry logic. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: Applications should tolerate dependency startup delays.

feedback.legit: Applications should tolerate dependency startup delays. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Applications should tolerate dependency startup delays. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

questions.prompt: How do containers discover each other on a user-defined Docker network?

answers.text: They can use Docker-provided name resolution for container or service names.

feedback.legit: They can use Docker-provided name resolution for container or service names. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: They can use Docker-provided name resolution for container or service names. The evidence supports the original reply.

answers.text: They must all publish ports to the public host interface.

feedback.legit: Publishing is unnecessary for container-to-container traffic on the shared network. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: Publishing is unnecessary for container-to-container traffic on the shared network. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: Network isolation controls which containers can communicate directly.

feedback.legit: Network isolation controls which containers can communicate directly. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: Network isolation controls which containers can communicate directly. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

questions.prompt: Why run a container process as a non-root user?

answers.text: It makes host kernel vulnerabilities impossible.

feedback.legit: Least privilege reduces impact but does not eliminate container escape risks. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: Least privilege reduces impact but does not eliminate container escape risks. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: A non-root process cannot listen on any network port.

feedback.legit: Non-root processes can listen on unprivileged ports and may receive narrowly configured capabilities when needed. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: Non-root processes can listen on unprivileged ports and may receive narrowly configured capabilities when needed. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: A Dockerfile USER instruction automatically changes ownership of files copied earlier.

feedback.legit: USER selects the account for later instructions and runtime; file ownership must be set separately, such as with COPY --chown. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: USER selects the account for later instructions and runtime; file ownership must be set separately, such as with COPY --chown. Your vote matches the correction.
